artifacts.content:
Hey Em,

Finally catching my breath and wanted to write everything down for you in one place instead of in a million texts.

The morning of Dad’s fall on January 9, 2026 really wrecked me. I was literally about to jump on that Acme Logistics demo when you texted that he’d been admitted to the ER at UCSF Medical Center. I tried to power through the 9:00 a.m. Zoom, but my head was half in the call and half in “okay, which one of us is taking which follow‑up appointments and physical therapy runs.” I rushed the close and never really slowed down to clarify procurement or legal sign‑offs for their pilot. I could hear myself interrupting their Director of CX and still couldn’t stop.

On top of that, I’m still technically on this informal performance plan from Elena after missing Q3 2025 quota by 38% and getting that written warning in October at the HelpFlow AI SF office. It’s made me weirdly conservative about pushing buyers to pull in IT or Support Ops because I’m scared of looking “too aggressive” and then losing the deal anyway. Not a great combo with worrying about Dad.

And honestly, I think the 0.5 mg alprazolam Dr. Amanda Ng at Kaiser Mission Bay had me take after the panic episode on January 3 left me a little foggy. It helped me sleep, but I definitely wasn’t as sharp on budget timing or stakeholder mapping as I needed to be.

Anyway, I can probably cover Dad’s PT on Tuesdays if you take the Friday UCSF visits. Let’s make a shared calendar so we don’t double‑book around Sophie’s daycare pickup.

Love,
Jordan

metadata.subject: Dad, work, and trying to juggle everything

artifacts.content:
Hey Grace,

You asked last night why I’ve been so intense about “sticking to the script” on calls lately, so here’s the long version.

I still think about that October 3, 2024 AcceleRoute demo where I broke down mid‑pitch after Mom Slacked that Emily had miscarried at UCSF Medical Center. I totally lost it on Zoom. Since then I’ve been forcing myself to run the discovery‑led flow exactly like the playbook so I don’t spiral if something blindsides me.

The hospital nights haven’t helped either. Remember January 12, 2025 at Zuckerberg San Francisco General? I was with Dad and the ER team until like 3 a.m. while they adjusted his insulin regimen for that mild diabetic complication. I went straight from there to the 10 a.m. logistics demo and basically leaned on the standard HelpFlow AI deck and the NorthStar Freight case study word‑for‑word just to get through it.

That’s why the six‑week public‑speaking group at the San Francisco Social Anxiety Clinic in November 2024 was such a big deal. Dr. Hernandez had us rehearse a 10‑minute product pitch every session, plus breathing and eye‑contact drills. I used those same techniques on the last Acme call and it actually felt…steady.

I’m also trying to be smarter about the meds. When Dr. Patel at Kaiser Mission Bay told me it was okay to take 0.5 mg alprazolam an hour before that January 7, 2025 demo for situational panic, it really did keep my heart rate down and my voice from cracking. But the 0.5 mg I took before the recent 9 a.m. call might’ve been too close to start time—I felt a bit foggy.

Thanks for putting up with all the late‑night deck rehearsals.

J

metadata.subject: Why I’ve been obsessing over my pitch

artifacts.content:
Personal work/therapy crossover reflections – updated Nov 22, 2024

1) Tightening explanations
I keep hearing Dr. Rao’s voice from our CBT sessions at UCSF Langley Porter last November: “Two sentences, max, then check if they’re with you.” She had me summarize my anxiety triggers in 1–2 sentences. I’ve started doing the same with our event‑streaming architecture—short version first, then offer a deeper SE call if they want it.

2) Better visuals/analogies
At home I’ve been rebuilding my deck using Dad’s liquor store in Oakland as my private mental model. Back in March 2023, after his mild stroke at Kaiser Oakland, it took him 10–12 weeks to trust the new POS/inventory system. That’s basically how long it takes customers to trust our AI suggestions. I’ll never mention his stroke or Kaiser on calls, but the story helps me explain the “trust curve” clearly.

3) Stronger pilot framing
Carla’s 90‑day remediation plan from July 2022 (after I missed Q2 by 38%) is still the best structure I’ve seen: weekly activity targets, written success criteria, Friday 3:30 p.m. reviews in the 3rd floor conference room at the HelpFlow SF office. I’m repurposing that as my default “90‑day pilot” narrative.

4) Adoption objections
When buyers worry their agents won’t adopt, I think about Ethan at the Port of Seattle in Feb 2021, almost getting written up for refusing the new scanning app. The 3‑week, 5‑person pilot group and daily check‑ins we set up is exactly the phased rollout I should propose.

Note to self: weave all four into next Acme Logistics call.

Event: SEA trip – Mom’s oncology consults at Swedish
Date: December 12–15, 2025
Location: Swedish Medical Center, Seattle / Parents’ house
Attendees: Me, Mom (Linda), Dad (Michael)

Notes:
- Fly SFO → SEA morning of 12/12, back late 12/15.
- Main purpose: be there for Mom’s early‑stage breast cancer consultations at Swedish Medical Center and help them process treatment options.
- Bring: laptop (will need to prep Acme Logistics “pilot readiness” materials on flight back), insurance paperwork folder, list of Mom’s meds.
- To‑dos with Mom and Dad:
  • Sit in on at least one consult to hear oncologist directly.
  • Review insurance coverage details and out‑of‑pocket estimates.
  • Help them fill out any Swedish/insurance forms.

Work impact:
- Block calendar for most meetings these days; keep only highest‑priority calls.
- I still owe Acme’s Director of Customer Experience a proper pre‑call email with security summary (SOC 2, DPA, standard MSA) and sample order form 24–48 hours before our next call. Realistically I’ll be drafting this on the 12/15 flight back to SFO, so add checklist:
  • Double‑check security FAQs.
  • Confirm Zendesk integration slide.
  • Outline procurement/legal steps clearly so I’m not scrambling on the call.

Personal:
- Schedule quick FaceTime with Grace each night.
- Text Emily updates so she doesn’t feel out of the loop.

metadata.title: Trip to Seattle – Mom’s Swedish consults
metadata.location: Swedish Medical Center, Seattle / SFO / Parents’ house

artifacts.content:
Hi Dad,

Just wanted to give you a real update instead of the rushed calls between meetings.

The CBT sessions with Dr. Anita Rao at UCSF Langley Porter last November have actually been bleeding into work in a good way. She had me practice boiling down my anxiety spirals into 1–2 sentences, and I’ve started doing the same thing when I talk through our architecture with buyers. It keeps me from rambling and losing them.

I’ve also been more careful about how I prep for big demos after that August 2023 QBR disaster when I showed up hungover from the DUI in SoMa the night before. I still think about how sloppy and rushed my value props were. Since then, no alcohol before important calls, period. The contrast with this week’s logistics demo was huge—pacing was controlled, and I actually followed the discovery‑led structure.

Money is still tight, though. I’m on a strict $600/month plan to pay down the $7,800 in credit card debt from flying to Seattle and staying near Harborview last summer when Sophie was hospitalized. Between that, rent at 2840 21st Street, Apt 5B, and helping Emily with a bit of daycare, I opened a $4,500 0% APR balance transfer with Chase back in November 2025 to keep us afloat.

I’ve been blocking off Tuesday/Thursday nights 7–9 p.m. since January to record Loom practice demos from the apartment. Funny story: I sent one to my old UW mentor, Professor Kim, and only later realized my 2023 tax form with my SSN was visible on the wall behind me. I re‑shot it immediately and took the form down.

Anyway, I’m trying to use all these screwups as fuel instead of just beating myself up.

Love,
Jordan

metadata.subject: Therapy, work, and trying to be smarter this time

artifacts.content:
Work/communication habits – tying home + calls together

1) Interrupting
Dr. Nguyen called me out in our April 15, 2024 couples session on 4th Street for cutting Grace off, especially since she started weekly anxiety sessions with Dr. Patel at UCSF Langley Porter in March. He said the same pattern shows up on sales calls. His homework: practice a full 3‑second pause before responding. I tried it on the May 7, 2024 logistics call and it actually helped me not jump in over their Support Lead.

2) Over‑talking from fear
Ever since Elena’s written warning on June 3, 2023 at the Market Street office about missing Q2 activity by ~18%, I’ve been front‑loading long monologues in discovery to prove I’ve “done my homework.” It’s backfiring—buyers talk less, rapport feels thinner. Need to get back to 50–60% buyer talk time.

3) Empathy from Dad’s stroke
Spending three weeks in the neurology wing at UCSF after Dad’s minor stroke on October 9, 2022 changed how I hear people. Watching nurses explain things slowly to Mom made me realize tone and pacing matter as much as content. I’ve started naming “change fatigue” explicitly when burned‑out support leaders talk about yet another tool.

4) Perspective from Sophie’s hospital bills
The $7,800 Harborview trip and the $600/month budget I’m on now are constant reminders that Ops/IT/Finance decisions hit real families. When a buyer mentions Finance, I try to ask who actually feels the impact if a tool fails or overruns budget.

Reminder: bring all of this into the next Acme Logistics discovery block.

Event: Tuesday Communication Skills Group – SF LGBT Center
Time: Tuesdays, 6:30–8:00 p.m.
Location: SF LGBT Center, San Francisco
Attendee: Jordan

Repeating weekly since January 2024.

Notes:
- Started going after Grace and I hit that rough patch around New Year’s. Group is run by Alex Kim. Format is 90‑minute sessions with a lot of role‑plays around listening, boundaries, and conflict.
- Core technique Alex pushes: wait 2 full seconds after the other person finishes, then paraphrase what they said before adding your own point. I tried this on the May 7, 2024 logistics prospect call when they were explaining their escalation process—“So what I’m hearing is Tier 1 pings Ops in Slack, then everything bottlenecks there, right?” It immediately opened them up more.
- Connects with what Dr. Nguyen said in couples therapy about my interrupting, and with how I’ve been over‑talking on calls since Elena’s June 3, 2023 written warning about low Q2 activity.

Pre‑group checklist:
- Text Grace if I’ll be late for dinner.
- Jot down one example from the week (work or home) where I either interrupted or successfully used the 2‑second pause.

Post‑group reflection (add after each session):
- Did I notice any crossover with sales calls (discovery, objection handling, stakeholder mapping)?
